topic: A homeowner learns their house was a former crime scene
raw_output:
[story 1]

The realtor’s cheerful pronouncements about "character" and "charm" grated on my nerves like sandpaper. I stood in the dusty foyer, my jaw tight, surveying the peeling wallpaper and the faint, almost invisible discoloration on the hardwood floor. This house, they claimed, had a story. I just saw decay and poor maintenance. I ran a finger across a patch of grime near the baseboard, the texture gritty under my nail. A faint, metallic tang seemed to cling to the air, something sharp and unpleasant. I pushed the front door open, the hinges groaning a protest that echoed the weariness in my own posture. It was just old, neglected property. Nothing more.

[story 2]

I surveyed the living room, my eyes sweeping over the faded floral patterns and the sagging sofa. A slow, deliberate sigh escaped my lips, a sound heavy with dismissal. Every detail felt staged, overly sentimental. Then, I noticed it—a slight unevenness in the plaster near the fireplace. I knelt down, brushing away a layer of settled dust. Beneath it, the surface looked subtly disturbed, a faint, unnatural shadow in the texture. My breath hitched, a sudden, cold tightening in my chest. I straightened up abruptly, my hands flexing at my sides, a distinct sense of profound irritation washing over me. This place was a mess, nothing more, but something about the silence felt wrong, heavy with unspoken history.

[story 3]

"Oh, look at the potential!" the agent chirped, oblivious to the rigid set of my shoulders. I didn't bother to look at the antique furniture; I was focused on the basement door, its wood warped and scarred. I pulled it open, and the smell hit me—not just mildew, but something chemical, stale, and deeply unsettling. My gaze fell on a dark stain seeping into the concrete near the foundation. I didn't need a forensic report to know what I was seeing. A careless, violent intrusion. I closed the door with a sharp click that sounded unnaturally loud in the quiet house. I walked back upstairs, my movements stiff, a deep furrow etched between my brows. This entire endeavor felt like a waste of time, a pointless engagement with someone else’s unpleasant narrative.
